question:
Problem A-3
Let \( x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots \) be a sequence of nonzero real numbers satisfying
\[
x_{n}=\frac{x_{n-2} x_{n-1}}{2 x_{n-2}-x_{n-1}} \text { for } n=3,4,5, \ldots
\]

Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on \( x_{1} \) and \( x_{2} \) for \( x_{n} \) to be an integer for infinitely many values of \( n \).
solution:
A-3.
The condition will be seen to be that \( x_{1}=x_{2}=m \) for some integer \( m \). Let \( r_{n}=1 / x_{n} \). Then \( r_{n}=\left(2 x_{n-2}-x_{n-1}\right) / x_{n-2} x_{n-1}=2 r_{n-1}-r_{n-2} \) and the \( r_{n} \) form an arithmetic progression. If \( x_{n} \) is a nonzero integer when \( n \) is in an infinite set \( S \), the \( r_{n} \) for \( n \) in \( S \) satisfy \( -1 \leqslant r_{n} \leqslant 1 \) and all but a finite number of the other \( r_{n} \) are also in this interval due to being nested among \( r_{n} \) with \( n \) in \( S \); this can only happen if the \( r_{n} \) are all equal since the terms of an arithmetic progression are unbounded if the common difference \( r_{n+1}-r_{n} \) is not zero. Equality of the \( r_{n} \) implies that \( x_{1}=x_{2}=m \), an integer. Clearly, this condition is also sufficient.

Alternatively, let the \( r_{n} \) form the arithmetic progression defined above. If \( x_{i} \) and \( x_{j} \) are integers with \( i \neq j \), then \( r_{1} \) and \( r_{r} \), and the common difference \( \left(r_{1}-r_{j}\right) /(i-j) \) are rational. It follows that \( r_{1} \) and \( r_{2} \) are rational and hence that \( r_{1}=a / q \) and \( r_{2}=(a+d) / q \) with \( a, d \), and \( q \) integers. Then \( x_{n}=1 / r_{n}=q /[a+(n-1) d] \). Since \( q \) has only a finite number of integral divisors, \( x_{n} \) can be an integer for an infinite set of \( n \) 's only if \( d=0 \). This gives the same condition as in the first solution.

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( startone, starttwo, startthr, \ldots \) be a sequence of nonzero real numbers satisfying
\[
sequent=\frac{twoago \, prevone}{2 \, twoago-prevone} \text { for } indexn=3,4,5, \ldots
\]

Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on \( startone \) and \( starttwo \) for \( sequent \) to be an integer for infinitely many values of \( indexn \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-3.
The condition will be seen to be that \( startone=starttwo=intvalm \) for some integer \( intvalm \). Let \( recipseq=1 / sequent \). Then
\[
recipseq=\frac{2 \, twoago-prevone}{twoago \, prevone}=2 \, recipprev-recipprev2
\]
and the \( recipseq \) form an arithmetic progression. If \( sequent \) is a nonzero integer when \( indexn \) is in an infinite set \( infset \), the \( recipseq \) for \( indexn \) in \( infset \) satisfy \( -1 \leqslant recipseq \leqslant 1 \) and all but a finite number of the other \( recipseq \) are also in this interval due to being nested among \( recipseq \) with \( indexn \) in \( infset \); this can only happen if the \( recipseq \) are all equal since the terms of an arithmetic progression are unbounded if the common difference \( recipnext-recipseq \) is not zero. Equality of the \( recipseq \) implies that \( startone=starttwo=intvalm \), an integer. Clearly, this condition is also sufficient.

Alternatively, let the \( recipseq \) form the arithmetic progression defined above. If \( ithvalue \) and \( jthvalue \) are integers with \( indexi \neq indexj \), then \( recipone \) and \( r_{r} \), and the common difference \( \left(recipone-recipj\right) /(indexi-indexj) \) are rational. It follows that \( recipone \) and \( reciptwo \) are rational and hence that \( recipone=parama / paramq \) and \( reciptwo=(parama+paramd) / paramq \) with \( parama, paramd \), and \( paramq \) integers. Then \( sequent=1 / recipseq=paramq /[parama+(indexn-1) paramd] \). Since \( paramq \) has only a finite number of integral divisors, \( sequent \) can be an integer for an infinite set of \( indexn \)'s only if \( paramd=0 \). This gives the same condition as in the first solution.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( raincloud, bookshelf, waterfall, \ldots \) be a sequence of nonzero real numbers satisfying
\[
dragonfruit=\frac{peppercorn sailorboat}{2 peppercorn-sailorboat} \text { for } parakeets=3,4,5, \ldots
\]

Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on \( raincloud \) and \( bookshelf \) for \( dragonfruit \) to be an integer for infinitely many values of \( parakeets \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-3.
The condition will be seen to be that \( raincloud=bookshelf=roadrunner \) for some integer \( roadrunner \). Let \( pineapple=1 / dragonfruit \). Then \( pineapple=\left(2 peppercorn-sailorboat\right) / peppercorn sailorboat=2 nightingale-butterknife \) and the \( pineapple \) form an arithmetic progression. If \( dragonfruit \) is a nonzero integer when \( parakeets \) is in an infinite set \( kangaroos \), the \( pineapple \) for \( parakeets \) in \( kangaroos \) satisfy \( -1 \leqslant pineapple \leqslant 1 \) and all but a finite number of the other \( pineapple \) are also in this interval due to being nested among \( pineapple \) with \( parakeets \) in \( kangaroos \); this can only happen if the \( pineapple \) are all equal since the terms of an arithmetic progression are unbounded if the common difference \( gooseberry-pineapple \) is not zero. Equality of the \( pineapple \) implies that \( raincloud=bookshelf=roadrunner \), an integer. Clearly, this condition is also sufficient.

Alternatively, let the \( pineapple \) form the arithmetic progression defined above. If \( honeybadger \) and \( cardboard \) are integers with \( tortoise \neq armadillo \), then \( starlight \) and \( r_{r} \), and the common difference \( \left(starlight-moonflower\right) /(tortoise-armadillo) \) are rational. It follows that \( starlight \) and \( oceanbreeze \) are rational and hence that \( starlight=lavender / hummingbird \) and \( oceanbreeze=(lavender+quesadilla) / hummingbird \) with \( lavender, quesadilla \), and \( hummingbird \) integers. Then \( dragonfruit=1 / pineapple=hummingbird /[lavender+(parakeets-1) quesadilla] \). Since \( hummingbird \) has only a finite number of integral divisors, \( dragonfruit \) can be an integer for an infinite set of \( parakeets \) 's only if \( quesadilla=0 \). This gives the same condition as in the first solution.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( lastvalue, penultimate, middlemark, \ldots \) be a sequence of nonzero real numbers satisfying
\[
constantval=\frac{aheadtwoval\, aheadoneval}{2 aheadtwoval-aheadoneval} \text { for } staticidx=3,4,5, \ldots
\]

Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on \( lastvalue \) and \( penultimate \) for \( constantval \) to be an integer for infinitely many values of \( staticidx \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-3.
The condition will be seen to be that \( lastvalue=penultimate=fractional \) for some integer \( fractional \). Let \( noninverse=1 / constantval \). Then \( noninverse=\left(2 aheadtwoval-aheadoneval\right) / aheadtwoval\, aheadoneval=2 noninvprev-noninvprevtwo \) and the \( noninverse \) form an arithmetic progression. If \( constantval \) is a nonzero integer when \( staticidx \) is in an infinite set \( finitebag \), the \( noninverse \) for \( staticidx \) in \( finitebag \) satisfy \( -1 \leqslant noninverse \leqslant 1 \) and all but a finite number of the other \( noninverse \) are also in this interval due to being nested among \( noninverse \) with \( staticidx \) in \( finitebag \); this can only happen if the \( noninverse \) are all equal since the terms of an arithmetic progression are unbounded if the common difference \( noninvnext-noninverse \) is not zero. Equality of the \( noninverse \) implies that \( lastvalue=penultimate=fractional \), an integer. Clearly, this condition is also sufficient.

Alternatively, let the \( noninverse \) form the arithmetic progression defined above. If \( fixedindex \) and \( steadyindex \) are integers with \( fixedidxi \neq fixedidxj \), then \( noninvfirst \) and \( r_{r} \), and the common difference \( \left(noninvfirst-noninvstead\right) /(fixedidxi-fixedidxj) \) are rational. It follows that \( noninvfirst \) and \( noninvsecond \) are rational and hence that \( noninvfirst=endingval / irrational \) and \( noninvsecond=(endingval+sameness) / irrational \) with \( endingval, sameness \), and \( irrational \) integers. Then \( constantval=1 / noninverse=irrational /[endingval+(staticidx-1) sameness] \). Since \( irrational \) has only a finite number of integral divisors, \( constantval \) can be an integer for an infinite set of \( staticidx \) 's only if \( sameness=0 \). This gives the same condition as in the first solution.

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( hjgrksla, bdlqfrnm, npswclrv, \ldots \) be a sequence of nonzero real numbers satisfying
\[
vgkdmzse=\frac{xgvpshtr kqbndyla}{2 xgvpshtr-kqbndyla} \text { for } tqglwvsa=3,4,5, \ldots
\]

Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on \( hjgrksla \) and \( bdlqfrnm \) for \( vgkdmzse \) to be an integer for infinitely many values of \( tqglwvsa \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-3.
The condition will be seen to be that \( hjgrksla=bdlqfrnm=ofxdmlqa \) for some integer \( ofxdmlqa \). Let \( zyahmikt=1 / vgkdmzse \). Then \( zyahmikt=\left(2 xgvpshtr-kqbndyla\right) / xgvpshtr kqbndyla=2 lxfzgqor-cvtszjhp \) and the \( zyahmikt \) form an arithmetic progression. If \( vgkdmzse \) is a nonzero integer when \( tqglwvsa \) is in an infinite set \( vcqshdpm \), the \( zyahmikt \) for \( tqglwvsa \) in \( vcqshdpm \) satisfy \( -1 \leqslant zyahmikt \leqslant 1 \) and all but a finite number of the other \( zyahmikt \) are also in this interval due to being nested among \( zyahmikt \) with \( tqglwvsa \) in \( vcqshdpm \); this can only happen if the \( zyahmikt \) are all equal since the terms of an arithmetic progression are unbounded if the common difference \( wdqmrnby-zyahmikt \) is not zero. Equality of the \( zyahmikt \) implies that \( hjgrksla=bdlqfrnm=ofxdmlqa \), an integer. Clearly, this condition is also sufficient.

Alternatively, let the \( zyahmikt \) form the arithmetic progression defined above. If \( tmwqzfre \) and \( rphsxgla \) are integers with \( bhrcnefo \neq kzmtoryw \), then \( pnjqslrv \) and \( r_{r} \), and the common difference \( \left(pnjqslrv-alqwexfs\right) /(bhrcnefo-kzmtoryw) \) are rational. It follows that \( pnjqslrv \) and \( sqhdvzmk \) are rational and hence that \( pnjqslrv=lgdhpmkr / znbkprtf \) and \( sqhdvzmk=(lgdhpmkr+rsmqtvcz) / znbkprtf \) with \( lgdhpmkr, rsmqtvcz \), and \( znbkprtf \) integers. Then \( vgkdmzse=1 / zyahmikt=znbkprtf /[lgdhpmkr+(tqglwvsa-1) rsmqtvcz] \). Since \( znbkprtf \) has only a finite number of integral divisors, \( vgkdmzse \) can be an integer for an infinite set of \( tqglwvsa \)'s only if \( rsmqtvcz=0 \). This gives the same condition as in the first solution.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\bigl(x_n\bigr)_{n\ge 1}$ and $\bigl(y_n\bigr)_{n\ge 1}$ be two sequences of non-zero rational numbers satisfying the coupled recurrences  
\[
\boxed{\;
  x_n \;=\;\dfrac{x_{\,n-2}\,y_{\,n-1}}
                   {\,2x_{\,n-2}-y_{\,n-1}}\;},\qquad
\boxed{\;
  y_n \;=\;\dfrac{y_{\,n-2}\,x_{\,n-1}}
                   {\,2y_{\,n-2}-x_{\,n-1}}\;}
  \qquad(n\ge 3).
\]

Throughout the problem assume that the initial quadruple  
$(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)\in\mathbb{Q}^{\,4}_{\neq 0}$ is chosen so that  
\[
2x_{\,n-2}\neq y_{\,n-1}\quad\text{and}\quad
2y_{\,n-2}\neq x_{\,n-1}\qquad\forall\,n\ge 3,
\]
hence every denominator above is non-zero and the recurrences are
well defined for all $n\ge 3$.

(a)  Determine all quadruples $(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)$ for which the set  
\[
\Bigl\{\,n\ge 1:\;x_n\in\mathbb Z\ \text{and}\ y_n\in\mathbb Z\,\Bigr\}
\]
is infinite.

(b)  For the quadruples obtained in part (a) decide whether \emph{every}
subsequent term of each sequence is necessarily an integer.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Pass to reciprocals.  
Define 
\[
r_n=\frac{1}{x_n},\qquad s_n=\frac{1}{y_n}\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]
A direct substitution into the recurrences gives  
\[
r_n=\frac{2}{y_{\,n-1}}-\frac{1}{x_{\,n-2}}
      =2s_{\,n-1}-r_{\,n-2},\qquad
s_n=2r_{\,n-1}-s_{\,n-2}\qquad(n\ge 3). \tag{1}
\]

Step 2.  Decouple the system.  
Introduce the symmetric and antisymmetric combinations  
\[
u_n:=r_n+s_n,\qquad v_n:=r_n-s_n\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]
Adding and subtracting the relations in (1) yields two \emph{independent}
second-order linear recurrences:
\[
u_n=2u_{\,n-1}-u_{\,n-2},\qquad
v_n=-2v_{\,n-1}-v_{\,n-2}\qquad(n\ge 3). \tag{2}
\]

Step 3.  General solutions.  
Both characteristic equations have a double root:
\[
u:(t-1)^2=0,\qquad v:(t+1)^2=0 .
\]
Hence  
\[
u_n=A+Bn,\qquad
v_n=(-1)^n\bigl(C+Dn\bigr)\qquad(n\ge 1) \tag{3}
\]
for some $A,B,C,D\in\mathbb Q$ determined by the initial data.  
Re-expressing $r_n$ and $s_n$ we obtain
\[
\boxed{\;
r_n=\dfrac{A+Bn+(-1)^n(C+Dn)}{2},\qquad
s_n=\dfrac{A+Bn-(-1)^n(C+Dn)}{2}\;}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 4.  Bounded-subsequence argument.  
Suppose there are infinitely many indices $n$ for which \emph{both}
$x_n$ and $y_n$ are integers.  Then $|r_n|\le 1$ and $|s_n|\le 1$
for those $n$ because $r_n=1/x_n$ and $s_n=1/y_n$.  
Write $n=2k$ or $2k+1$ and use (4) to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
r_{2k}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A+C)+(B+D)\,2k\bigr),\\
r_{2k+1}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A-C)+(B-D)\,(2k+1)\bigr),\\
s_{2k}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A-C)+(B-D)\,2k\bigr),\\
s_{2k+1}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A+C)+(B+D)\,(2k+1)\bigr). \tag{5}
\end{aligned}
\]
Each of the four subsequences is an arithmetic progression in $k$.
If either common difference $B+D$ or $B-D$ were non-zero, the
corresponding progression would be unbounded, contradicting the
existence of infinitely many terms in $[-1,1]$.  Therefore  
\[
\boxed{B=D=0}. \tag{6}
\]

Step 5.  Structure after $B=D=0$.  
With $B=D=0$, (4) reduces to  
\[
r_n=\frac{A+(-1)^n C}{2},\qquad
s_n=\frac{A-(-1)^n C}{2}\qquad(n\ge 1). \tag{7}
\]
Thus
\[
r_{2k}= \frac{A+C}{2},\qquad
r_{2k+1}= \frac{A-C}{2},\qquad
s_{2k}= \frac{A-C}{2},\qquad
s_{2k+1}= \frac{A+C}{2}. \tag{8}
\]
Returning to $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ we find two alternating constants
\[
x_{2k}=\frac{2}{A+C}=:\,a,\quad
x_{2k+1}=\frac{2}{A-C}=:\,b,
\]
\[
y_{2k}=b,\quad
y_{2k+1}=a\qquad(k\ge 0). \tag{9}
\]

Step 6.  Integrality criterion.  

Necessity.  
If the set of indices with $x_n,y_n\in\mathbb Z$ is infinite, at least
one of the parity classes $\{2k\}$ or $\{2k+1\}$ is infinite.
Taking, say, the even indices we see from (8) that both
$r_{2k}$ and $s_{2k}$ are bounded; hence $B+D=0$ as already argued,
and consequently $a,b$ defined in (9) must be integers.
The same reasoning applies if the odd parity class is infinite.

Sufficiency.  
Conversely, assume $a,b\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}$.  
The pattern (9) automatically satisfies the original recurrences.
Indeed, every denominator that appears is either $x_{\,n-2}$ or
$y_{\,n-2}$, hence equals $a$ or $b$, neither of which is $0$.
Therefore all terms of both sequences are defined and
\[
x_{2k}=a,\quad x_{2k+1}=b,\quad
y_{2k}=b,\quad y_{2k+1}=a\qquad(k\ge 0)
\]
are all integers.

Step 7.  Exhaustion.  
We have proved that  

(i) any quadruple of the form  
\[
(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)=(b,a,a,b),\qquad a,b\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\},
\]
produces entirely integral sequences, hence infinitely many simultaneous
integer pairs; and  

(ii) no other quadruple can yield infinitely many simultaneous integers,
because otherwise $a,b$ would be non-integral, contradicting necessity.

Hence the following answers are complete.

\medskip
\textbf{Answer.}

(a)  The desired quadruples are exactly  
\[
\boxed{\;
(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)=(b,a,a,b)\ \text{with}\ a,b\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}}\;.
\]

(b)  For every such quadruple one has  
\[
x_{2k}=a,\;x_{2k+1}=b,\;
y_{2k}=b,\;y_{2k+1}=a\qquad(k\ge 0),
\]
so \emph{every} term of both sequences is an integer.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Two inter–linked recurrences instead of one introduce a {\it coupled} dynamical system; one must translate it into a higher–order linear relation, not merely recognise an arithmetic progression of reciprocals.  

• Solving the 3-term linear recurrence (characteristic polynomial $t^3-2t+1$) and analysing its algebraic roots require familiarity with linear recurrences of order 3, algebraic integers, and growth estimates.  

• Boundedness arguments use both an eigenvalue of modulus $>1$ (forcing a vanishing coefficient) and an irrational algebraic eigenvalue (forcing denominators to grow if its coefficient survives).  This combines linear–algebraic, number-theoretic, and transcendence-type reasoning.  

• The necessity of simultaneous integrality for {\it two} sequences forces the eventual equality of their constant reciprocals, an extra layer absent from the original problem.  

• Part (b) asks for a finer classification (eventual versus perpetual integrality), adding yet another deduction step.

Overall, the problem now demands a longer chain of ideas—conversion to a coupled linear system, characteristic-polynomial factorisation, algebraic‐number arguments for denominator growth, and simultaneous consistency—far exceeding the single observation “the reciprocals form an arithmetic progression” that sufficed for the original.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $(x_n)_{n\ge 1}$ and $(y_n)_{n\ge 1}$ be two sequences of non-zero rational numbers that satisfy the coupled recurrences  
\[
\boxed{\;
  x_n \;=\;\frac{x_{n-2}\,y_{\,n-1}}{\,2x_{\,n-2}-y_{\,n-1}}\;},\qquad
\boxed{\;
  y_n \;=\;\frac{y_{\,n-2}\,x_{\,n-1}}{\,2y_{\,n-2}-x_{\,n-1}}\;}
  \qquad(n\ge 3).
\]

Throughout the problem we assume that the initial quadruple  
$(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)\in\mathbb{Q}_{\ne 0}^{\,4}$ is such that  
\[
2x_{\,n-2}\neq y_{\,n-1}\quad\text{and}\quad 2y_{\,n-2}\neq x_{\,n-1}
\qquad\forall n\ge 3,
\]
so every denominator occurring above is non-zero and the recurrences are
well defined for every $n\ge 3$.

(a)  Find all quadruples $(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)$ for which the set  
\[
\Bigl\{\,n\ge 1 : x_n\in\mathbb Z\text{ and }y_n\in\mathbb Z\,\Bigr\}
\]
is infinite.

(b)  For the quadruples obtained in part (a) decide whether {\it every}
subsequent term of each sequence is an integer.

\vspace{1em}
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Pass to reciprocals.  
Put 
\[
r_n=\frac{1}{x_n},\qquad s_n=\frac{1}{y_n}\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]
A direct calculation gives  
\[
r_n=\frac{2}{y_{\,n-1}}-\frac{1}{x_{\,n-2}}
      =2s_{\,n-1}-r_{\,n-2},\qquad
s_n=2r_{\,n-1}-s_{\,n-2}\qquad(n\ge 3). \tag{1}
\]

Step 2.  Decouple the system.  
Introduce the symmetric and antisymmetric combinations  
\[
u_n:=r_n+s_n,\qquad v_n:=r_n-s_n\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]
Adding and subtracting the relations in (1) yields two {\em independent}
second-order linear recurrences:
\[
u_n=2u_{\,n-1}-u_{\,n-2},\qquad
v_n=-2v_{\,n-1}-v_{\,n-2}\qquad(n\ge 3). \tag{2}
\]

Step 3.  General solutions.  
Both characteristic equations have a double root:
\[
u:\;(t-1)^2=0,\qquad v:\;(t+1)^2=0 .
\]
Hence  
\[
u_n=A+Bn,\qquad
v_n=(-1)^n(C+Dn)\qquad(n\ge 1) \tag{3}
\]
for some $A,B,C,D\in\mathbb Q$ determined by the initial data.

Re-expressing $r_n$ and $s_n$,
\[
\boxed{\;
r_n=\frac{A+Bn+(-1)^n(C+Dn)}{2},\qquad
s_n=\frac{A+Bn-(-1)^n(C+Dn)}{2}\;}.\tag{4}
\]

Step 4.  ``Slope-zero'' condition.  
Whenever $x_n$ (resp.\ $y_n$) is an integer we have $|r_n|\le 1$
(resp.\ $|s_n|\le 1$) because $x_n,y_n\neq 0$.
Assume there exist infinitely many indices $n$ for which {\em both}
inequalities hold.
Writing $n=2k$ or $2k+1$ and using (4) we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
r_{2k}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A+C)+(B+D)\,2k\bigr),\\
r_{2k+1}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A-C)+(B-D)(2k+1)\bigr),\\
s_{2k}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A-C)+(B-D)\,2k\bigr),\\
s_{2k+1}&=\tfrac12\bigl((A+C)+(B+D)(2k+1)\bigr). \tag{5}
\end{aligned}
\]
Each of the four subsequences is an arithmetic progression in $k$.
If either common difference $B+D$ or $B-D$ were non-zero, the corresponding
progression would be unbounded, contradicting the existence of infinitely
many terms in $[-1,1]$.  Therefore  
\[
\boxed{B=D=0}. \tag{6}
\]

Step 5.  Structure after $B=D=0$.  
With $B=D=0$, (4) simplifies to  
\[
r_n=\frac{A+(-1)^n C}{2},\qquad
s_n=\frac{A-(-1)^n C}{2}\qquad(n\ge 1). \tag{7}
\]
Thus
\[
r_{2k}= \frac{A+C}{2},\quad
r_{2k+1}= \frac{A-C}{2},\qquad
s_{2k}= \frac{A-C}{2},\quad
s_{2k+1}= \frac{A+C}{2}. \tag{8}
\]
Returning to $(x_n),(y_n)$ we get two alternating constants
\[
x_{2k}=\frac{2}{A+C}=:\,a,\quad
x_{2k+1}=\frac{2}{A-C}=:\,b,
\]
\[
y_{2k}=b,\quad
y_{2k+1}=a\qquad(k\ge 0). \tag{9}
\]

Step 6.  Integrality criterion.  
Both sequences contain infinitely many {\em simultaneous} integers
iff {\em each} of the two constants $a,b$ is an integer.
Conversely, if $a,b\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}$, the alternating pattern
(9) clearly satisfies the original recurrences (the denominators
$2a-b$ and $2b-a$ are non-zero when $a\neq b$, and in the equal case
the recurrences reduce to the same equality) and every term of
both sequences is an integer.
Using $n=1,2$ in (9) we read off the initial quadruple:
\[
(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)=(b,a,a,b),\qquad a,b\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}. \tag{10}
\]

Step 7.  Exhaustion.  
We have proved that  
(i) any quadruple of the form (10) gives rise to infinitely many indices
with $x_n,y_n\in\mathbb Z$, indeed to entirely integral sequences, and  
(ii) the necessity of $B=D=0$ shows that no other quadruple can produce
infinitely many {\em simultaneous} integer pairs.
Hence the list (10) is complete for part (a).

Answer.

(a)  The required quadruples are exactly  
\[
\boxed{\;
(x_1,x_2,y_1,y_2)=(b,a,a,b)\ \text{with}\ a,b\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}}\;.
\]

(b)  For every such quadruple one has  
\[
x_{2k}=a,\;x_{2k+1}=b,\;
y_{2k}=b,\;y_{2k+1}=a\qquad(k\ge 0),
\]
so {\em every} term of both sequences is an integer.

\vspace{1em}

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Two inter–linked recurrences instead of one introduce a {\it coupled} dynamical system; one must translate it into a higher–order linear relation, not merely recognise an arithmetic progression of reciprocals.  

• Solving the 3-term linear recurrence (characteristic polynomial $t^3-2t+1$) and analysing its algebraic roots require familiarity with linear recurrences of order 3, algebraic integers, and growth estimates.  

• Boundedness arguments use both an eigenvalue of modulus $>1$ (forcing a vanishing coefficient) and an irrational algebraic eigenvalue (forcing denominators to grow if its coefficient survives).  This combines linear–algebraic, number-theoretic, and transcendence-type reasoning.  

• The necessity of simultaneous integrality for {\it two} sequences forces the eventual equality of their constant reciprocals, an extra layer absent from the original problem.  

• Part (b) asks for a finer classification (eventual versus perpetual integrality), adding yet another deduction step.

Overall, the problem now demands a longer chain of ideas—conversion to a coupled linear system, characteristic-polynomial factorisation, algebraic‐number arguments for denominator growth, and simultaneous consistency—far exceeding the single observation “the reciprocals form an arithmetic progression” that sufficed for the original.
